title: Congress Still Trading Stocks in 2026 — Why a Ban Keeps Failing Despite 76% Public Support
description: Despite overwhelming bipartisan public support, congressional stock trading bans have failed repeatedly. Here's who's trading, how much they're making, and why reform keeps stalling.
lede: Three years after the most recent push to ban congressional stock trading, members of Congress are still actively buying and selling individual stocks — and many are still outperforming the market. Polls consistently show 70-76% of Americans support a trading ban, yet every proposed bill has died in committee. The question isn't whether the system is broken. It's why nobody in power wants to fix it.

faqs.q: Can members of Congress trade stocks?
faqs.a: Yes. Members of Congress are legally allowed to trade individual stocks. The STOCK Act of 2012 requires them to disclose trades within 45 days, but there is no ban on trading itself. The penalty for late disclosure is only $200.

faqs.q: Do Congress members outperform the S&P 500?
faqs.a: Studies have found that certain members of Congress, particularly those on committees overseeing industries they invest in, consistently outperform market averages. Nancy Pelosi's portfolio has been one of the most scrutinized examples.

faqs.q: Why hasn't Congress banned stock trading for its members?
faqs.a: Despite broad public support (70-76% in polls), proposed bills like the TRUST Act and ETHICS Act have repeatedly failed to advance. Critics argue that members who benefit from the current system have little incentive to change it.

The Stop Trading on Congressional Knowledge Act (2012) requires members of Congress to publicly disclose stock trades within 45 days. It prohibits using non-public information for trading, but enforcement has been minimal and the $200 fine for late disclosure is widely criticized as toothless.

faqs.q: How can I track what stocks Congress members are buying?
faqs.a: Several free tools track congressional stock disclosures: Quiver Quantitative, Capitol Trades, and Unusual Whales all parse the public filings. Our Nancy Pelosi Stock Tracker provides analysis of the most-watched congressional portfolio.

sections.heading: The $200 Fine That Protects Millions in Trades
paragraphs: The STOCK Act, passed in 2012, was supposed to bring transparency to congressional trading. It requires members to disclose all stock transactions within 45 days. The penalty for missing the deadline? A $200 fine — roughly what most Americans spend on groceries in a week.

In practice, dozens of members have filed late disclosures with no meaningful consequence. The 45-day window itself creates an information asymmetry: by the time the public learns what a Congress member bought, the trade is already six weeks old. For options trades — which some members favor for their leverage — that delay can mean the difference between a 200% gain and a flat market.

sections.heading: The Most-Watched Portfolio in Washington
paragraphs: Nancy Pelosi's portfolio, managed by her husband Paul Pelosi (a venture capitalist), has become the most-tracked congressional portfolio in America. The Pelosis have made substantial trades in Nvidia, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Broadcom, and Palo Alto Networks — many of which overlapped with periods when Pelosi sat on or chaired committees overseeing the tech industry.

Social media has turned Pelosi into an unlikely investing icon. Multiple apps and Twitter bots now track her disclosures in real time. 'Pelosi tracker' ETF proposals have been floated (though none have launched). The irony: a lawmaker who opposes a trading ban has become the strongest argument for why one is needed.

Track the Pelosi portfolio's historical trades, estimated returns, and sector allocation with our Nancy Pelosi Stock Tracker — including a copy-trade calculator that shows what you'd have made following each disclosed trade.

sections.heading: It's Not Just Pelosi
paragraphs: While Pelosi gets the headlines, she's far from the only active congressional trader. Senator Markwayne Mullin (R-OK), Representative Dan Crenshaw (R-TX), and former Senator Tommy Tuberville (R-AL) have all faced scrutiny for aggressive trading activity. The issue is genuinely bipartisan — both parties have members who trade actively and both parties have members pushing for reform.

According to Quiver Quantitative's data, dozens of members traded more than $1 million in individual stocks in 2025 alone. Some of these trades occurred in sectors directly regulated by committees those members serve on — a pattern that academic researchers have flagged as statistically unlikely to be coincidental.

sections.heading: Why Every Ban Proposal Has Failed
paragraphs: At least four major bills have been proposed since 2022 to ban or severely restrict congressional stock trading: the TRUST Act, the ETHICS Act, the Ban Congressional Stock Trading Act, and various bipartisan compromises. All have failed to reach a floor vote.

The structural problem is simple: the people who would need to vote for the ban are the same people who benefit from the status quo. Leadership in both chambers has consistently declined to prioritize trading reform legislation, even when individual members co-sponsor it. Without leadership support, bills die in committee regardless of their bipartisan backing.

The most likely path to reform may not come from Congress at all. State-level ballot initiatives, SEC rulemaking, or executive orders could potentially restrict trading without requiring congressional self-regulation — though each approach faces its own legal and political challenges.
